title: Part I: Jacques and the Beast
paragraphs: Jacques Lantier was an engine driver on the Paris-Le Havre line. He had driven the same locomotive—engine 608, which he called La Lison—for three years, and he knew her as well as he knew himself. He could tell from the sound of her pistons, from the feel of her speed beneath him, whether she was running well or badly.

He was a handsome young man of twenty-six, dark-haired, with good features—features that would have been entirely pleasant if not for a certain something in his eyes. A darkness. A shuttered quality. The other drivers noticed it but could not name it.
The darkness had a name. Jacques knew its name. He called it the Beast.

It came upon him suddenly, without warning—this savage desire to kill. He could be walking normally, speaking normally, and then he would look at a woman—any woman, a stranger on the platform—and feel within him a convulsion of murderous rage. He had never acted on it. He had learned, through years of frightened self-control, to recognize the first signs and to get away from people, to find some private place until the fit passed.

He did not know where it came from. He suspected some ancestral heredity—some taint passed down through generations of drunkenness and violence. His family, the Lantiers: his father had been a drunkard, and his uncle, and he himself did not drink because he had discovered that even a little alcohol made the Beast stir.

He loved La Lison instead. When he was in his cab, moving at speed through the French countryside, the locomotive alive beneath him, the steam hissing and the great pistons working—he felt calm. The Beast was still.
He had found an equilibrium. He would not kill anyone. He would be a good engine driver and live alone and grow old in his cab, and the Beast would sleep.
Then he met Séverine.

title: Part II: The Crime at the Station
paragraphs: The stationmaster at La Croix-de-Maufras was named Roubaud. He was a thick-set, jealous man who suspected his young wife of everything and was sometimes right to suspect her.

Séverine was twenty-five, with large clear blue eyes and a child-like quality that concealed a great deal. She had been the ward of a man named Grandmorin—a wealthy judge, a respectable figure—and what had happened between her and Grandmorin when she was young was a thing she tried not to think about.
Then one day, in a fit of jealous rage, Roubaud got the truth out of her. And rather than leave her or forgive her, he did something worse: he made her help him.

They murdered Grandmorin together. In a first-class compartment of the Paris-Le Havre express, traveling at night through the Norman countryside. Roubaud held the old man while Séverine watched, and then Roubaud threw the body from the moving train.
Jacques was driving the express that night. He saw something—a shape in the darkness—and felt, in the very instant of seeing, the Beast stir in him as it had never stirred before.
He said nothing. He drove on. He delivered his passengers to Le Havre and signed his reports and went home.
But Séverine had seen him too—had looked up as the express roared past La Croix-de-Maufras and seen the driver's face in the lamplight. And she thought: he saw.

The investigation into Grandmorin's death dragged on and pointed, briefly, at various suspects. Roubaud was anxious. Séverine, more controlled, did what was necessary. She went to Paris and introduced herself to the investigating judge and smiled at him with her clear blue eyes.
She also sought out Jacques Lantier.
She told him she knew he had seen something. She asked him not to speak. She looked at him with those eyes.
He did not speak. But he fell in love with her.

title: Part III: The Beast Unbound
paragraphs: Jacques and Séverine became lovers. He thought: this time is different. She is different. He could be with her without feeling the Beast stir. He held her in his arms and felt something he had never felt—tenderness, safety, calm.
She told him everything. About Grandmorin, about Roubaud, about the murder in the train. She wept as she told him, and he held her. She asked him: do you still love me?
'Yes,' he said. He meant it.
She said: I want to be free of Roubaud. I want to start again. With you.

He understood what she was asking. She did not say the word. She did not need to. He held her and looked out the window at the railway line—the shining rails in the moonlight, running on forever—and understood what she was asking.
He planned it carefully. He chose a night when Roubaud would be working the late shift. He went to the station at La Croix-de-Maufras. He waited.
But when the moment came—when he stood over Roubaud in the darkness with the knife in his hand—the Beast came.
Not for Roubaud. For Séverine.

He did not understand it, even as it happened. He had loved her, genuinely. But the Beast did not distinguish between love and its opposite—it only knew the blade in his hand and a woman's throat and the convulsion of savage fury that had been waiting all his life.
He killed Séverine. He stood over her afterward and could not explain what he had done.
He ran. He went back to his locomotive, La Lison. He drove.

La Lison was destroyed in a collision that winter—a head-on crash caused by a defective signal. Jacques, at the controls, did not attempt to brake. Some said afterward that it seemed as though he had driven straight into the wreck deliberately.
The engine was destroyed. Jacques was killed. The Beast was still at last.

The other locomotives continued up and down the Paris-Le Havre line. The investigation into Grandmorin's death was eventually closed, with inconclusive results. Roubaud, suspected of nothing in particular, went on working.
The line continued. The engines ran. The Beast slept in the earth—or in the rails—or in whatever part of human nature it had always inhabited.
It would wake again.
